text: So the operation I'm going to show you is an operation I did at the end of twenty seventeen. The patient is a thirty three year old female who is five foot eight inches tall and weighs three hundred twenty six pounds. Her BMI is fifty. She lost twenty pounds in preparation for this operation.
Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon retracts the liver to reveal the stomach, confirming the absence of a hiatal hernia.

This actually I think is the most important part of the operation and that is planning out exactly where I'm going to divide across the stomach while the stomach is still in its initial undisturbed form. As soon as I start manipulating the stomach by having the anesthesiologist pass a tube down to the stomach or by dividing the attachments along the side of the stomach or by pulling on the stomach to position it for the stapler, it causes distortion of the normal anatomy of the stomach because the stomach is very elastic, very stretchy. And if I staple across a distorted stomach, I get a distorted sleeve.

The surgeon uses a felt pen to draw precise lines on the stomach in its undisturbed state, marking surgical boundaries. A measuring tape is used to ensure accuracy, with a lower vertical line positioned five centimeters above the stomach's bottom and an upper horizontal line marking the angularis. The marked area below the angularis is four centimeters wide, while above it, the width is set between two and a half to three centimeters.

Using these dimensions of four centimeters wide below the angularis and two and a half centimeters to three centimeters wide above the angularis, I will end up with a small volume sleeve of three to four ounces in volume. In my experience, this is ideal. Any smaller and there can be too much restriction, any larger and weight loss isn't as good. this three to four ounce volume will expand to approximately six to eight ounces over time.
The surgeon divides the attachments along the greater curvature of the stomach, progressing towards the diaphragm to prepare for the gastric division.

The surgeon divides the attachments along the greater curve of the stomach, visible on the right side of the screen, freeing the back wall of the stomach. The lesser curve is visible on the left side of the stomach.
The surgeon has completed freeing the entire greater curve of the stomach. The anesthesiologist inserts a 36 French bougie tube, measuring 12 millimeters in diameter, into the distal stomach.

The surgeon applies a Cavidian stapler with a black cartridge to the marked line on the stomach, accommodating the thicker tissue at the bottom. The stapler is fired, creating a precise staple line that maintains the intended width of four centimeters.

The surgeon applies the stapler with a black cartridge to the stomach, ensuring the staple line is equidistant from the lesser curve along both the back and front walls of the sleeve. Careful manipulation of the stomach is performed to maintain the precise alignment of the staple line.

The surgeon's right hand operates the stapler to fire staples along the edge of the gastric sleeve, forming a precise staple line. The assistant's right hand is visible on the right side of the screen, while the assistant's left hand holds the camera. The surgeon's left hand is positioned in the upper left of the frame.
The surgeon measures the upper part of the gastric sleeve above the level of the angularis, marked by a horizontal purple line, aiming for a width of two and a half to three centimeters.

The surgeon positions the initial staple firing to start two to six centimeters from the bottom of the stomach, aiming for a sleeve width of four centimeters at the bottom to achieve an overall sleeve volume of three to four ounces.

The surgeon uses a purple cartridge in the stapler to measure and staple the stomach along the greater curve, ensuring the sleeve is two and a half to three centimeters wide. The purple line marks the desired width to prevent distortion during stapler application, aiming for a sleeve under three centimeters for increased post-surgical restriction.

The surgeon marks the upper part of the stomach with the bougie in place to identify the lesser curve, which is obscured by fat. The stapler is positioned along the purple line to create a sleeve approximately two and a half centimeters wide. The assistant holds the stomach to ensure precise stapler placement. The staple line appears straight and aligned with the lateral margin of the sleeve. The surgeon checks the open jaw width of the stapler, confirming it is two and a half centimeters wide before firing.
The surgeon performs an additional firing of the stapler to complete the division of the stomach, ensuring the staple line is properly aligned along the edge of the sleeve.
The surgeon reinforces the top part of the staple line with sutures, visibly pulling the stomach tissue over the staple line to reduce tension and lower the risk of leaks during healing.

The surgeon removes the gastrogrammat through the abdominal wall and performs a leak test by occluding the sleeve distally while the anesthesiologist injects air to distend the sleeve. The surgeon uses a tape measure to verify the dimensions of the sleeve, confirming the upper part is between two and a half and three centimeters wide, with the angularis at three centimeters and the antrum at four centimeters wide. The staple line begins four centimeters before the pylorus. The final inspection shows the sutured portion is intact, with no bleeding or hiatal hernia, and the sleeve appears symmetric with a straight staple line.

If you'd like to see this operation uncut in its entirety, you can find it at the OLLIAC Center YouTube channel. If you'd like to learn more about our practice or read more about patient outcomes after weight loss surgery, you can visit us online at www.olliaccenter.com.
